title: Pleopeltis pteropus
intro: Pleopeltis pteropus, commonly known as the resurrection fern, is a type of epiphytic fern native to tropical regions of Central and South America. The plant is prized for its potential health benefits, including its antioxidant and anti-inflammatory properties, which may aid in reducing stress and improving cardiovascular health. The bioactive constituents of Pleopeltis pteropus include saponins, flavonoids, and phenolic acids, which contribute to its medicinal properties. The most common herbal preparations made with the resurrection fern include teas, capsules, and tinctures, often marketed for their adaptogenic and stress-relief benefits.
taxonomy: Pleopeltis pteropus is a species of epiphytic fern in the family Polypodiaceae. It is commonly found in tropical and subtropical regions of Central and South America. The classification of Pleopeltis pteropus is as follows: kingdom Plantae, division Pteridophyta, class Polypodiopsida, order Polypodiales, family Polypodiaceae, genus Pleopeltis, and species P. pteropus. This classification reflects the plant's evolutionary relationships and its position within the fern family. The specific classification of Pleopeltis pteropus provides a clear understanding of its botanical identity.
uses: Pleopeltis pteropus, also known as the flying monkey fern, has been studied for its potential medicinal properties. It is believed to have anti-inflammatory and antioxidant effects, which could contribute to its traditional use in treating various health issues. The plant's extracts have been shown to possess antimicrobial properties, which may aid in wound healing and the treatment of infections. Additionally, studies suggest that Pleopeltis pteropus may have neuroprotective effects, making it a subject of interest for research into potential treatments for neurodegenerative diseases. Further research is needed to fully understand the medicinal uses and potential benefits of this plant.

side_effects: Pleopeltis pteropus is also known as the flying vine or flying Dutchman's pipe, and it's native to the southeastern United States.  When used in folk medicine, it has been reported to have various side effects, including skin irritation, allergic reactions, and gastrointestinal issues. Ingesting the plant can cause nausea, vomiting, and diarrhea due to its toxic compounds. Furthermore, it may also cause respiratory problems, such as coughing and wheezing, in some individuals. It is essential to exercise caution when handling or consuming Pleopeltis pteropus due to its potential risks.
